← the Atlas

Welcome, revamped — pin it, reach it, run agents

feature · seedling ·implemented ·JUN 05, 2026

A bird's-eye in-app welcome plus a full kolu.dev/welcome guide. The spine — the same `tailscale serve` that makes kolu reachable from your phone gives it the HTTPS secure context that unlocks one-click PWA install (you can still pin manually over plain HTTP). One command, both wins. Plan of record.

Contents

  • The spine: one command unlocks two features
    • Verified facts (the plan rests on these)
  • The in-app welcome
    • The PWA card is context-aware
  • The full guide — kolu.dev/welcome
  • Building it
    • Architecture — through the electricity lens
    • Build plan
    • Pitfalls
    • Decisions

Today, kolu has no welcome — only an empty state. When the canvas holds zero terminals, packages/client/src/EmptyState.tsx renders a session-restore card and a list of keyboard shortcuts. PWA install shows up once, passively, as a single ambient tip (packages/client/src/settings/tips.ts:85) that’s hidden the moment you’re already installed. Remote use and Tailscale appear nowhere.

This note proposes a bird’s-eye in-app welcome (a revamp of that empty state, re-openable on demand) and a full guide at kolu.dev/welcome — and threads both around one verified insight.

kolu · welcome
Terminals on an infinite canvas. Any agent.
Three things worth doing first.
📌
Pin it
Install kolu as an app — its own window, dock icon, and live agent badge.
PWA
🌐
Reach it anywhere
One Tailscale command and kolu follows you to your phone, over real HTTPS.
Tailscale
🤖
Run agents
Open a repo, drop a tile, launch Claude / Codex / OpenCode. Agent-agnostic.
Canvas
New terminal ⌘⏎  ·  Palette ⌘K
Full guide → kolu.dev/welcome
Closed it? ⌘K → "Tutorial" brings it back anytime.

The spine: one command unlocks two features

kolu’s two onboarding goals — pin it as an app and reach it from anywhere — look separate. They are the same action.

A self-hosted kolu is almost always served over plain HTTP on a LAN (192.168.x.x) or Tailscale (100.x) address. You can still pin it from there — Chrome’s Create shortcut → Open as window and iOS Safari’s Add to Home Screen both work over http:// — but the frictionless one-click install (the beforeinstallprompt prompt / omnibox install icon) and the OS app badge need a secure context (HTTPS, or the loopback/localhost set). The very same tailscale serve that makes kolu reachable from your phone gives it a real https://…ts.net origin — so it unlocks the one-click install and remote access in one command. Tell it as one story.

Plain HTTP
http://100.x:PORT — manual pin only, off-LAN unreachable
→
tailscale serve
tailscale serve --bg PORT
→
✓ Real HTTPS
https://box.tailnet.ts.net
↳ secure context → 1-click install + app badge
↳ on your tailnet → Reachable anywhere

Verified facts (the plan rests on these)

Four load-bearing claims, each adversarially fact-checked against current (2025–26) primary sources.

Plain HTTP: manual install works, one-click doesn't

Only the automatic path needs a secure context (W3C Secure Contexts §3.1: HTTPS, or the loopback/localhost set). Over http:// on a 192.168.x.x LAN IP or a Tailscale 100.x IP, Chromium’s beforeinstallprompt / omnibox install icon won’t fire, and the OS app badge (Badging API) and service workers are unavailable. That part is real.

But you can still pin kolu manually over plain HTTP — the common self-hosted case, and what most users actually do (MDN, web.dev):

  • Chrome / Edge desktop: ⋮ → Save and share → Create shortcut… → tick Open as window (a windowed app; no manifest install needed). Confirmed working on macOS Chrome over http.
  • iOS Safari: Share → Add to Home Screen — no HTTPS requirement; launches standalone via the manifest’s display. Confirmed working on iPhone Safari over http.

So window.isSecureContext gates which affordance to offer, not whether install is possible: on an insecure origin, show manual instructions (and recommend HTTPS for the one-click prompt + app badge) — never a dead one-click button, and never “you can’t install.”

  • Tailscale Serve = a genuine secure context. tailscale serve reverse-proxies a local port and terminates TLS at https://machine.tailnet.ts.net with a real Let’s Encrypt cert (DNS-01 against *.ts.net; private key never leaves your box) — browsers accept it with no warnings, so install + service workers unlock. confirmed Caveats: enable MagicDNS + HTTPS Certificates once in the admin console; the full FQDN is mandatory (the bare hostname and the 100.x IP are still plain HTTP); Serve is tailnet-only; the FQDN lands in public Certificate-Transparency logs, so don’t bake secrets into machine names. (Serve KB, HTTPS KB)
  • beforeinstallprompt is Chromium-only. Chrome/Edge/Brave/etc. fire it; Firefox and Safari (desktop and iOS) never do — and Chrome/Edge on iOS run on WebKit, so they don’t fire it either. A custom JS Install button must start hidden and reveal only on the event. confirmed
  • iOS has no install API. The only path is the manual Share → Add to Home Screen, which works regardless of any event (since iOS 16.4 from Chrome/Edge/Firefox too, not just Safari). On iOS you render instructions, not a button. Requires HTTPS + display: standalone + an apple-touch-icon; avoid the deprecated apple-mobile-web-app-capable meta. confirmed

Manifest enrichment — in scope for this work, not 'later'

The manifest is served standalone with 192/512 icons, now assembled by @kolu/surface-app’s installPwaManifest ( #1154 , in master), whose ...extra passthrough already accepts the missing fields — so enriching it is just passing description and a maskable 512px icon (logo inside the safe zone on the brand background) at the call site (packages/server/src/index.ts:223). No screenshots: they only prettify the install card (install works without them) and committed product shots go stale as the UI moves — not worth the maintenance. Validate via DevTools → Application → Manifest — Lighthouse’s PWA audit is deprecated.

The in-app welcome

Revamp EmptyState in place rather than add a modal. The patterns worth stealing: VS Code Walkthroughs (a short, re-openable checklist — not a forced tour; it explicitly warns against excessive steps), Warp (one welcome surface, success measured as habit not completion), Zed (empty-state-as- welcome, re-openable from the palette), and Raycast’s EmptyView (the empty state carries the onboarding with actions).

The moments (cap ~3–5, hard). The three cards above — Pin it, Reach it anywhere, Run agents — sit above the existing restore card and shortcut list. Each is one verb + one line + one chip; depth lives behind the “Full guide” link, not inline. Lean on kolu’s auto-detection (recent repos, recent agents in packages/client/src/wire.ts) so the welcome shows the user’s own data, reinforcing zero-setup.

Re-openable via the palette. EmptyState only auto-shows at zero terminals, so the welcome would vanish forever once you’re working. Add a stable palette command — labelled “Tutorial” (alias “Welcome”) — that re-summons it anytime, mirroring VS Code’s openWalkthrough. Wire it as an action in packages/client/src/input/actions.ts and surface it through the registry in packages/client/src/commands.tsx.

No dismiss state — by design. Zero terminals always shows the welcome. The way you “dismiss” it is to open a terminal; open zero again and it comes right back. There is no welcomeSeen flag, no “Don’t show again,” nothing to persist, nothing to go stale — the empty canvas is the trigger, full stop. The only addition is the “Tutorial” palette command, which re-summons the same welcome (as an overlay) even while you’re working. Register a feature-discoverability tip pointing at it (per .claude/rules/conventions.md) so the re-open is discoverable.

The PWA card is context-aware

This is where the spine pays off. The card reads the runtime and renders one of three states — it never shows a button that can’t work:

http:// (insecure)
Pin it manually
You're on http://box:7777. Use the browser menu (Create shortcut → Open as window) or Add to Home Screen. HTTPS via Tailscale adds one-click + a badge.
How → Reach it anywhere
https + Chromium
Pin kolu to your dock
Its own window, app icon, and a live badge for finished agents.
Install
iOS Safari
Add to Home Screen
Tap Share ⬆ , then “Add to Home Screen”. (Instructions — iOS has no install button.)
Show me

Gate order (apply top-down — the first match wins):

1. already installed            → render nothing
     matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)').matches
     || navigator.standalone === true
2. secure + beforeinstallprompt → real one-click Install button (Chromium)
3. secure, no event             → per-platform manual instructions (table below)
4. !window.isSecureContext      → manual-install instructions (browser menu /
     Add to Home Screen) + recommend HTTPS via Tailscale for one-click + badge

Per-platform instruction set (render the literal glyph as inline SVG next to numbered steps — visual beats prose):

Platform / browserWhat kolu shows
Chromium desktop / Android, event capturedReal Install button → prompt() in the click handler
Android Chrome (no event)Menu ⋮ → Install app
Android FirefoxMenu → Install (no event, but install works)
iOS — Safari / Chrome / Edge / Firefox (16.4+)Illustrated Share ⬆ → Add to Home Screen (instructions, not a button)
Safari desktop (macOS Sonoma / Safari 17+)File → Add to Dock (works on any page; no event)
Firefox desktopNative install not shipped yet (experimental Taskbar Tabs) — suggest Chrome/Edge
Any non-secure origin (http://)Manual-pin steps — Chrome Create shortcut → Open as window, iOS Add to Home Screen — plus “HTTPS via Tailscale adds one-click + a badge.” Never a dead one-click button

Fact-check (June 2026): non-Chromium still installs — just not via JS

The “beforeinstallprompt is Chromium-only” claim is accurate about the event — Firefox and Safari never fire it — but that is not “no install,” so kolu must instruct per browser rather than dead-end on “use Chrome”:

  • Safari desktop (macOS Sonoma / Safari 17+): File → Add to Dock turns any page into a web app — no manifest or event needed. (Apple)
  • Safari iOS / iPadOS: Share → Add to Home Screen (since iOS 16.4 also from Chrome / Edge / Firefox).
  • Firefox Android: menu → Install.
  • Firefox desktop: no native install yet — Taskbar Tabs is experimental in Nightly (browser.taskbarTabs.enabled), not GA; suggest Chrome/Edge. (MDN)

The @khmyznikov/pwa-install component already renders the Safari/iOS instruction screens, so the detection branch picks the right one.

Library decision: use @khmyznikov/pwa-install, wrap thinly in Solid

A ~28 kB, MIT, framework-agnostic web component (~41k weekly downloads, kept current for iOS 18+) that already solves the parts we’d otherwise re-implement: the iOS/macOS Share-sheet instruction screens, localization, standalone detection, one-shot prompt(), and the appinstalled listener. This matches kolu’s “prefer well-maintained libraries over custom code” rule (.claude/rules/conventions.md). Wrap the custom element in a SolidJS component, feed it manifest-url / icon / install-description, hand it the captured event via externalPromptEvent, and read isInstallAvailable / isUnderStandaloneMode. Hand-rolling is only worth it for Chromium-only with fully bespoke branding — which is not the goal. Ship the wrapper as a new package, packages/solid-pwa-install (@kolu/solid-pwa-install), a focused SolidJS adapter alongside solid-pierre / solid-markdown / solid-fileview — so the install card is reusable by other surface apps (drishti included), not buried in kolu’s client. (repo)

The existing tip-gate (packages/client/src/settings/useTips.ts:14) already filters amb-pwa-install when installed. Its copy points at the browser menu, which does work over plain HTTP — so the tip stays useful on an insecure origin; only drop it once actually installed.

The full guide — kolu.dev/welcome

A new website/src/pages/welcome.astro (Astro + Tailwind, alongside the existing index.astro). Structure it as a Raycast-style skill ladder, each section copy-pasteable and ending at a verifiable success state:

  1. Intro what / who — the canvas + agent-agnostic thesis, one line.
  2. Quickstart basics — Nix install → running kolu, preserving the README’s literal first block; ends at “you should see kolu open with an empty canvas.”
  3. First 5 minutes the win — open a repo → launch an agent in a tile (mirrors in-app moment #3).
  4. Core concepts core — canvas, tiles, dock, worktrees, command/sub-palette, auto-detection.
  5. Pin it as an app (PWA) core — the per-OS steps from above, with the HTTP/secure-context caveat called out up front and Tailscale as the fix.
  6. Reach it anywhere (Tailscale) power — the exact minimal sequence (below), the Serve-vs-Funnel safety note, and the decision aid.
  7. Power features power — multi-agent at scale, keybindings, sessions.
  8. FAQ / Troubleshooting — lead with “I see no Install button” → you’re on HTTP, use the ts.net URL.

The page is built to grow. Beyond the essentials, deeper how-tos live in collapsible sections (<details>, collapsed by default) — Using the code browser, Working the canvas view, Worktrees, Sessions, … — added over time without lengthening the first read or breaking the scannable skill ladder.

Every in-app card and empty-state hint deep-links to the matching anchor here; this page stays the single source of truth, kept in sync with in-app copy and the README (per .claude/rules/workflow.md).

The Tailscale walkthrough (the one to get exactly right)

  1. Install Tailscale on the dev box and the phone/laptop; sign both into the same account.
  2. Admin console → DNS: enable MagicDNS, then HTTPS Certificates (one-time; acknowledge the public-ledger notice).
  3. On the dev box: tailscale serve —bg <PORT> (—bg survives reboots).
  4. Open the printed https://<machine>.<tailnet>.ts.net on the phone — install the PWA from there.

Default to Serve (tailnet-only), never Funnel (public, no auth — a footgun; a single flag flips a port public). Keep the dev server on 127.0.0.1 and let Serve be the only exposed surface. Manage with tailscale serve status / reset / off. Decision aid: Serve = “just my devices”; Cloudflare Tunnel + Access = “public + identity-gated”; ngrok = “throwaway public URL”. Rule out Caddy local-CA / self-signed — its root isn’t trusted on a remote phone, so no secure context.

Frictionless payoff: when kolu can resolve its own Serve URL, surface it in-app with a copy button so handing it off is one tap.

Building it

Architecture — through the electricity lens

Read against electricity.mdx: infrastructure that ① is domain-agnostic, ② hides a hard volatility, and ③ graduates to a second consumer earns its own @kolu/* package; everything else stays kolu domain. That splits this work into three layers.

① @kolu/surface-app #1154 — the app-shell electricity, now in master. Already owns the manifest (installPwaManifest, whose ...extra passthrough absorbs the enrichment above) and the headless “relationship-to-server” model (useSurfaceApp(): connection lifecycle, build-skew). Installability — window.isSecureContext + standalone display-mode — is a sibling environment fact, so the canInstallPwa / isInstalled signals belong in that same headless model. Graduates today (drishti is the second consumer).

② @kolu/solid-pwa-install — a new electricity. A focused SolidJS adapter wrapping @khmyznikov/pwa-install, sibling to solid-pierre / solid-markdown / solid-fileview. Behind one socket (“install this web app”) it owns the cross-browser install volatility — beforeinstallprompt capture, the per-platform instruction screens, appinstalled — reading installability from ①. Passes all three tests: agnostic (no terminal/canvas/git), hides a genuinely hard volatility (every browser installs differently), graduates (any surface app). @kolu/surface-app may itself depend on @kolu/solid-pwa-install and compose the card with its own installability signals — so a consumer wires install exactly once. Supersedes the earlier “wrap it inside the kolu client” idea.

③ kolu app — domain, not electricity. The welcome itself is kolu’s story:

AreaFileChange
In-app welcomepackages/client/src/EmptyState.tsxThe 3 moments + the @kolu/solid-pwa-install card, above restore/shortcuts
Re-open commandpackages/client/src/input/actions.ts, packages/client/src/commands.tsxNew “Tutorial” action + palette entry — re-summons the welcome as an overlay
Discoverability tippackages/client/src/settings/tips.ts, packages/client/src/settings/useTips.tsTip pointing at “Tutorial”; drop the PWA tip on an insecure origin. No welcomeSeen — zero terminals always shows it
Manifest call-sitepackages/server/src/index.ts:223Pass description + maskable icon into installPwaManifest (no screenshots — they go stale)
Full guidenew website/src/pages/welcome.astroThe skill-ladder page

No new server contract; no service worker (kolu has none by design).

Built on @kolu/surface-app (merged)

@kolu/surface-app ( #1154 ) is in master, so layers ① and ② build on it directly — installPwaManifest and the useSurfaceApp() headless model are already there to extend.

Build plan

  • ▸ Manifest enrichmentPass description and a safe-zone maskable icon into @kolu/surface-app’s installPwaManifest (no screenshots — they only prettify the card and go stale).
  • ▸ Installability signalsAdd canInstallPwa (isSecureContext) + isInstalled (display-mode / navigator.standalone) to the useSurfaceApp() headless model.
  • ○ @kolu/solid-pwa-installNew package wrapping @khmyznikov/pwa-install: gate order, per-platform branch, beforeinstallprompt + appinstalled wiring.
  • ○ EmptyState revampThe 3 moments + the install card. The most visible change.
  • ○ Tutorial command + tipPalette command that re-summons the welcome; discoverability tip. No persistence.
  • ○ kolu.dev/welcomeThe skill-ladder guide; deep-link anchors from in-app cards; <details> sections grow over time.
  • ○ Tailscale URL surfacingResolve + show the ts.net URL with a copy button.

Pitfalls

Onboarding anti-patterns

  • Dead Install buttons on HTTP. The #1 kolu-specific trap — gate on isSecureContext first, always.
  • Default-visible custom Install button. Safari/Firefox/iOS never fire the event; hide by default, reveal on event.
  • Treating the iOS card as a button. It’s instructions; it can’t trigger anything.
  • Long forced tours. kolu’s audience hates hand-holding — one win, then links. (VS Code warns against this explicitly.)
  • Replaying onboarding for returning users — “the airplane safety video every flight.” Persist + detect returning + offer re-entry.
  • Funnel by default, or the bare hostname / 100.x IP. Funnel is public+unauthenticated; the bare host and IP are plain HTTP. Always the full ts.net FQDN, Serve not Funnel.
  • Reproducing docs in the panel. Verb + one line + one action inline; depth links out, or the two copies drift.

Decisions

  • No mobile welcome. Onboarding is desktop-only — mobile already opts out of ambient tips via showsAmbientTips (packages/client/src/capabilities.ts:34).
  • No persistence. Zero terminals always shows the welcome; opening a terminal is the dismissal, and it returns whenever you’re back at zero. No welcomeSeen, nothing to go stale.
  • Tailscale: instruct, never auto-run. Even when tailscale is on PATH, the welcome documents the command — it does not offer to run tailscale serve for you.
  • No QR. A copy button on the ts.net URL is enough.
  • Install ownership. @kolu/solid-pwa-install owns the card UI + cross-browser install volatility; @kolu/surface-app owns the manifest + installability signals and may depend on solid-pwa-install to compose the card — a surface app wires install once.
  • Builds on #1154 (merged). @kolu/surface-app is in master; layers ① and ② extend its installPwaManifest and useSurfaceApp() directly.

Status: implemented in #1199 .

← the Atlas github ↗
